title: Warburg’s Werewolf: An Anamnesis
summary: Aby Warburg spent his life finding forms that could hold their own against the flow of time. All the while, as Kevin Dann explores, he was churning on the brink of madness with the sense that he himself was changing — into a terrifying animal. What kind of history would a werewolf write?
full_text: In the four years since his breakdown in November 1918, Aby Warburg had never stopped exhibiting wolfish behaviors. Along with the capricious violence regularly unleashed on nurses, doctors, and other attendants, and prolonged bouts of shouting and screaming, Warburg’s delusions, nearly all of them visual hallucinations, centered on eating, defecating, “territory” — the defense of his personal space — and other primitive, animalistic aspects of existence. He imagined that he was a cannibal, his food was poisoned, people around him were really ghosts, and that others had animal heads and teeth. He continued to hallucinate that either he or proximal criminal conspirators had killed his wife, children, and other family members, whose dismembered corpses lay in neighboring buildings.
